claims.claim: The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) produces collision energies that are significantly lower than those reached by high-energy cosmic rays bombarding the Earth and other astronomical bodies.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: LHC design collision energy per beam
claim_quantities.source_quote: designed to collide pairs of protons each with energies of 7 TeV

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Energy threshold for cosmic rays exceeding LHC energy
claim_quantities.source_quote: if its energy reaches or exceeds 10^8 GeV, i.e., 10^17 eV

claims.importance_rationale: This provides the fundamental empirical basis for safety: if nature has tolerated higher energies without catastrophe, the LHC is inherently safer.
claims.relevance_rationale: This provides the fundamental empirical basis for safety: if nature has tolerated higher energies without catastrophe, the LHC is inherently safer.
claims.source_quote: The energies of accelerators have been increasing regularly over the past decades, though they are still far below those of the most energetic cosmic rays.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: LHC design collision energy per beam
claim_quantities.source_quote: designed to collide pairs of protons each with energies of 7 TeV

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Energy threshold for cosmic rays exceeding LHC energy
claim_quantities.source_quote: if its energy reaches or exceeds 10^8 GeV, i.e., 10^17 eV

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: LHC collision energies are much lower than those of cosmic rays that have bombarded Earth for billions of years.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The LHC's operation presents no conceivable threat because the energies achieved are significantly lower than those observed in cosmic-ray collisions with astronomical bodies, which have remained stable for billions of years.

supporting_quotes.quote: The energies of accelerators have been increasing regularly over the past decades, though they are still far below those of the most energetic cosmic rays.

claims.claim: Microscopic black holes produced at the LHC are expected to decay via Hawking radiation before reaching detector walls, and even if stable, they would not accrete matter in a dangerous manner.
claims.importance_rationale: Addresses the specific concern of black hole production and their potential for runaway growth.
claims.relevance_rationale: Addresses the specific concern of black hole production and their potential for runaway growth.
claims.source_quote: Any microscopic black holes produced at the LHC are expected to decay by Hawking radiation before they reach the detector walls.

stated_limitations: requires violation of basic physical principles for stability/suppressed decay

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Microscopic black holes would decay quickly or, if stable, lack the mechanism to grow dangerously.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The LHC's operation presents no conceivable threat because the energies achieved are significantly lower than those observed in cosmic-ray collisions with astronomical bodies, which have remained stable for billions of years.

supporting_quotes.quote: Any microscopic black holes produced at the LHC are expected to decay by Hawking radiation before they reach the detector walls.
the decay rate would be directly related to the production rate, and the expected lifetime would be very short.

claims.claim: The continued existence of the Earth and other astronomical bodies (like the Sun and Moon) provides empirical evidence that high-energy collisions do not produce dangerous vacuum bubbles or magnetic monopoles.
claims.importance_rationale: Uses the survival of celestial bodies as a 'natural experiment' to rule out catastrophic vacuum decay or monopole catalysis.
claims.relevance_rationale: Uses the survival of celestial bodies as a 'natural experiment' to rule out catastrophic vacuum decay or monopole catalysis.
claims.source_quote: The continued existence of the Universe means that such vacuum bubbles are not produced in cosmic-ray collisions, and hence the LHC will also not produce any vacuum bubbles.

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: The Earth and Sun have survived billions of years of cosmic-ray bombardment, proving that such collisions do not trigger vacuum decay or dangerous magnetic monopoles.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The LHC's operation presents no conceivable threat because the energies achieved are significantly lower than those observed in cosmic-ray collisions with astronomical bodies, which have remained stable for billions of years.

supporting_quotes.quote: The continued existence of the Universe means that such vacuum bubbles are not produced in cosmic-ray collisions, and hence the LHC will also not produce any vacuum bubbles.

The continued existence of the Earth and other astronomical bodies after billions of years of high-energy cosmic-ray bombardment means that any monopoles produced could not catalyze proton decay at any appreciable rate.

excluded_as_not_decision_relevant: Specific technical details of the RHIC experimental setup (e.g., specific detector names)
Detailed mathematical derivations of the penalty factor for A=20 nuclei
Historical context of 1980s/1990s experiments unless directly supporting safety conclusions
